retrieved_docs.content: Complications are difficult to ascertain due to the lack of studies with adequate control groups. Maternal: perineal lacerations, vaginal lacerations, and hematomas, anal sphincter injury, long term complication of pelvic organ prolapse. [13] Fetal: facial lacerations, facial nerve injury, ocular trauma, skull fracture, intracranial hemorrhage, subgaleal hematoma, hyperbilirubinemia, fetal death. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The use of forceps in infant delivery has fallen out of favor among obstetricians in the past three decades. Forceps deliveries now make up about only 1.1% of vaginal deliveries (according to a retrospective cohort involving more than 22 million vaginal deliveries from 2005-2013). [1] The use of vacuum extraction has also declined but is more frequently used compared to forceps delivery; this may be because vacuum extraction is easier to use in comparison to forceps. However, vacuum use is less likely to result in a successful vaginal delivery when compared to the use of forceps. [2] Despite the increased efficacy compared to the vacuum, forceps use is still on the decline. Some speculate that this downward trend is due to fear of litigation related to common complications of forceps delivery, including increased risk of perineal laceration and injury to the newborn. Others attribute the decreased use of forceps to lack of adequate resident training. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Forceps Delivery -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Describe the indications for forceps delivery. Review the complications of forceps delivery. Summarize the contraindications for forceps delivery. Outline the importance of collaboration and coordination among the interprofessional team to recognize the indications for forceps delivery, ensuring patient safety and improving outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: The routine use of episiotomy is not supported; therefore, the World Health Organization and ACOG recommend its restricted use. [8] [1] Routine episiotomy was debated, with advocates claiming it could prevent unexpected severe tears. [3] However, studies demonstrated that not all women suffer significant perineal trauma during vaginal birth, and the routine use of episiotomy exposes patients to unnecessary surgical incisions and their associated complications. Even in emergent cases (eg, shoulder dystocia or instrumental-assisted deliveries), the evidence does not consistently support episiotomy’s ability to prevent severe tears. In some cases, severe perineal tears were found to occur despite episiotomy. Therefore, the selective use of episiotomy is recommended based on specific clinical considerations rather than routine practice. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Perineal Lacerations -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Following vaginal delivery, 9 of 10 women experience some form of perineal trauma either spontaneously or secondary to episiotomies. [7] The incidence of perineal trauma between countries varies due to different definitions of perineal trauma classification and underdiagnosis. [2] Most perineal lacerations that occur in a vaginal delivery can be classified as first- or second-degree. Of these lacerations, 60% to 70% will require suturing. [8] The incidence of OASIS injuries occurs in approximately 4% to 11% of deliveries in the United States. [3] With each additional birth, the frequency and severity of perineal trauma decreases. [3] .

retrieved_docs.title: Perineal Lacerations -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetric perineal lacerations, frequently occurring during childbirth, can affect the perineum, labia, vagina, and cervix. While most lacerations heal without long-term issues, severe cases can result in prolonged pain, sexual dysfunction, and embarrassment. These severe lacerations need prompt identification and proper repair during delivery. Perineal trauma is common in vaginal births, with lacerations occurring spontaneously or due to medical interventions like episiotomies. The majority of these lacerations are first- or second-degree and usually require suturing. However, severe lacerations, particularly obstetric anal sphincter injuries, which occur in approximately 4% to 11% of vaginal deliveries in the United States, can lead to significant complications like pelvic floor dysfunction and incontinence. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Although intended to widen the vaginal opening and reduce perineal trauma during delivery, episiotomy carries risks such as perineal pain, dyspareunia, and sexual dysfunction. Complications include bleeding, infection, extended tears into the anal sphincter, scarring, and even more serious outcomes like vaginal prolapse or fistulas. In some cases, severe perineal tears occur despite episiotomy, raising questions about its effectiveness in preventing trauma. Episiotomy has also been associated with complications during future deliveries. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Perineal Lacerations -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: An episiotomy is a surgical procedure performed at the bedside during the second stage of labor, which causes enlargement of the posterior vagina. [12] This is done just before the delivery to decrease maternal blood loss. An episiotomy may be indicated if there is a need for expedited delivery of the fetus, soft tissue dystocia, or a need to aid an operative vaginal delivery. [6] [8] [12] The 2 most common types of episiotomies are midline and mediolateral. [12] The midline episiotomy is the most commonly performed type in the United States and is associated with a higher frequency of severe perineal lacerations. [6] [8] [12] The mediolateral episiotomy is more difficult to repair and is associated with increased postpartum pain and blood loss. The routine use of episiotomy is not supported; therefore, the World Health Organization and ACOG recommend its restricted use. [6] [8] Please see StatPearls' companion resource, " Episiotomy ," for more information.

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Despite its benefits in some cases, the routine use of episiotomy has been debated. Research demonstrates that episiotomy may not reduce the severity of pelvic floor issues or improve long-term outcomes. Instead, episiotomy can increase the risk of anal incontinence and other complications, particularly if the incision extends into an OASIS injury. Selective use of episiotomy compared with its routine use during vaginal birth is associated with lower rates of posterior perineal trauma, less suturing, and fewer healing complications. Because of the severe complications that may result from this procedure or an inadequately repaired episiotomy, an in-depth knowledge of the appropriate indications, proper technique, and correct repair is essential to optimizing patient outcomes. Furthermore, preventative measures, including perineal massage in late pregnancy and intrapartum perineal support, should also be implemented to reduce the need for episiotomy. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Equipment
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetric anal sphincter injuries : Regional or general anesthesia may be required. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Episiotomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The long-term effects studied in follow-up research regarding episiotomy revealed no difference in urinary issues, anal incontinence, or painful intercourse (ie, dyspareunia) between the spontaneous lacerations and episiotomy groups. Despite this, evidence remains limited, and larger trials are needed to clarify the role of episiotomy in complex deliveries. [7] Subsequently, the  American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) has stated that no definite indications for episiotomy have been established. Consequently, the current recommendation is to utilize restrictive episiotomy, in which episiotomy is considered based on clinical factors, including nonreassuring fetal heart tones and assisted vaginal births with vacuum or forceps, particularly in nulliparous patients. In these cases, studies have shown that mediolateral episiotomy significantly reduces the risk of obstetrical anal sphincter injuries. [9] [11]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2516
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery constitute a broad category of obstetric interventions that are employed to facilitate vaginal birth. Within this category, the specific complication فشل التوليد بالملقط (failure of forceps delivery) is identified as a child procedure, indicating that it is a particular adverse outcome associated with assisted delivery techniques. Episiotomy, a surgical incision made in the perineum to enlarge the vaginal opening, functions as a parent procedure within the same group, linking various delivery‑assisting methods and serving as a foundational technique for related interventions. Preventive incision, exemplified by episiotomy, is recognized as having a direct relationship to these obstetric procedures, underscoring the role of episiotomy as a preventive measure designed to reduce trauma during delivery and to support the successful execution of other assisted delivery methods. Together, these concepts illustrate how episiotomy and preventive incisions are integral components of the spectrum of procedures used to assist delivery, while also highlighting specific complications such as فشل التوليد بالملقط that can arise from assisted vaginal birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 2253
retrieved_docs.content: Low forceps delivery with episiotomy is a specific type of assisted vaginal delivery that falls under the broader category of forceps, vacuum, and breech delivery procedures. This obstetric technique, which involves the use of forceps and an episiotomy incision, is closely associated with بضع الفرج (perineal tears), highlighting the importance of careful perineal management and appropriate selection of delivery methods to minimize maternal trauma and optimize outcomes. Understanding that Low forceps delivery with episiotomy is both a subset of assisted delivery techniques and linked to perineal injury informs clinicians’ decisions regarding operative vaginal births and postoperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1536
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy is identified as a specific therapeutic procedure within the domain of obstetric care, being categorized under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد. This surgical incision of the perineum, performed during the second stage of labor, is considered one of the specialized interventions used to facilitate vaginal delivery and protect surrounding tissues. Recognizing episiotomy as part of the broader set of obstetric therapeutic procedures underscores its role in managing childbirth complications and informs clinical decision‑making regarding when and how to employ this technique for optimal maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2387
retrieved_docs.content: Other procedures inducing or assisting delivery is a broad obstetric category that encompasses the specific outcome of فشل التوليد بالملقط (forceps delivery failure). Within this framework, الولادة بالملقط (forceps delivery) is directly linked to فشل التوليد بالملقط, indicating that the failure represents a particular adverse event or subcategory of the forceps delivery procedure. Consequently, both الولادة بالملقط and its associated failure, فشل التوليد بالملقط, are situated under the larger grouping of other procedures that induce or assist delivery, highlighting their interrelated roles in assisted vaginal birth and the importance of recognizing potential complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2791
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy and تمزق الأغشية الاصطناعي (artificial membrane rupture) are both classified under الإجراءات العلاجية الخاصة بالتوليد (obstetric therapeutic procedures). By recognizing these interventions as subcomponents of the broader category of obstetric therapeutic care, clinicians can better organize treatment options, understand their related indications, and apply appropriate management strategies within the field of obstetrics.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1666
retrieved_docs.content: Episiotomy, a preventive incision performed during vaginal delivery, is closely linked to its subsequent repair; the repair of episiotomy is classified as a specific type of episiotomy procedure and is grouped within the broader category of other procedures associated with delivery. Recognizing that preventive incision (episiotomy) and its repair are interrelated steps in managing perineal trauma helps clinicians organize delivery‑related interventions and provide appropriate postpartum care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1152
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrics incision includes the specific entity بضع الفرج, which represents a perineal tear as a type of surgical incision performed in obstetric care. The procedure of low forceps delivery with episiotomy is closely linked to بضع الفرج, indicating that this delivery technique may result in or involve this particular perineal incision. Consequently, بضع الفرج is understood both as a subclass of obstetrics incisions and as an outcome associated with low forceps delivery accompanied by episiotomy, highlighting its relevance in the management of maternal perineal trauma during assisted vaginal births.
